did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper uses a Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) based Transfer Learning model with VGG-16 for skin cancer detection. The model's hyperparameters are optimized using a proposed Fractional Gazelle Optimization Algorithm (FGOA). The study utilizes a dermatology dataset to train the AI model and evaluate its performance in classifying skin cancer.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental work involving the development of a novel Fractional Gazelle Optimization Algorithm (FGOA) integrated with a Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) based Transfer Learning model using VGG-16 for skin cancer detection. It details specific methods including data preprocessing, feature selection, and model training, and reports original experimental results with performance metrics (accuracy, TPR, TNR, PPV, NPV) on a Dermatology Dataset, comparing them against other techniques.

title: A novel skin cancer Detection based transfer learning with optimization algorithm using Dermatology Dataset
journal: EAI Endorsed Transactions on Pervasive Health and Technology
brief_summary: This study proposes a novel skin cancer detection method using a Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) based Transfer Learning model with VGG-16, optimized by a proposed Fractional Gazelle Optimization Algorithm (FGOA). The FGOA integrates Fractional Calculus and the Gazelle Optimization Algorithm to perform feature selection on the Dermatology Dataset from the UCI repository. Data preprocessing involved missing value imputation and linear normalization before the optimized model was trained. The model's performance was evaluated using accuracy, True Positive Rate (TPR), True Negative Rate (TNR), Positive Predictive Value (PPV), and Negative Predictive Value (NPV). The proposed FGOA_CNN-based TL with VGG-16 achieved superior results compared to comparative techniques, attaining maximal values of 92.65% accuracy, 91.48% TPR, 93.56% TNR, 90.35% NPV, and 90.77% PPV.

lesion_types_studied.details: The paper focuses on the detection of skin cancer using a Dermatology Dataset from the UCI repository. While the introduction mentions specific types such as melanoma, basal cell carcinoma, and squamous cell carcinoma, the experimental classification task is binary (cancerous vs. non-cancerous) or general skin cancer detection using clinical and histopathological features, rather than multi-class lesion segmentation or specific subtype classification.
Mentioned in the introduction as a severe kind of skin cancer and a focus for early detection, but not explicitly listed as a distinct target class in the experimental dataset description or results.
Mentioned in the introduction as one of the three common types of skin cancer, but not explicitly listed as a distinct target class in the experimental dataset description or results.
Mentioned in the introduction as one of the three common types of skin cancer, but not explicitly listed as a distinct target class in the experimental dataset description or results.

all_task_types.details: The paper performs binary classification to detect skin cancer using a CNN-based Transfer Learning model with VGG-16. The target is determining whether skin data is cancerous or not, evaluated using metrics such as Accuracy, True Positive Rate (TPR), True Negative Rate (TNR), Positive Predictive Value (PPV), and Negative Predictive Value (NPV).

datasets_used.name: Dermatology Dataset
datasets_used.description: A dataset from the UCI repository used for skin cancer detection. It contains clinical and histopathological features (34 hyperparameters and 33 linear valued features) used to diagnose various skin diseases. The study notes that diagnosis often requires biopsy for histopathological features, and the database includes features like scaling and erythema.

use.training: Used for training the CNN-based Transfer Learning model with VGG-16, optimized by the Fractional Gazelle Optimization Algorithm (FGOA). The study varied training data percentages (e.g., 70%, 80%, 90%) for comparative assessment.
use.validation: Used for validation via k-fold cross-validation (specifically mentioned as k-fold data of 9 in the discussion).
use.testing: Used for testing to evaluate performance metrics (Accuracy, TPR, TNR, PPV, NPV) against comparative techniques.
use.other: Used for feature selection via the proposed FGOA.

statistical_methods_used.purpose: Used for comparative assessment of the proposed FGOA_CNN-based TL with VGG-16 against other techniques (LSI with CFS, DOLHGS, Multi-scale structure, RegNetY-320) to evaluate performance metrics (Accuracy, TNR, TPR, NPV, PPV) with varying k-fold values (e.g., k=7, 8, 9).

statistical_methods_used.method: Comparative analysis based on varying training data percentages
statistical_methods_used.purpose: Used to evaluate the robustness and performance of the proposed model compared to baseline techniques by testing with different proportions of training data (e.g., 70%, 80%, 90%).

statistical_methods_used.method: Algorithmic analysis based on varying swarm size
statistical_methods_used.purpose: Used to assess the sensitivity of the optimization algorithms (WOA, EHO, LOA, GOA, FGOA) to hyperparameter settings (swarm size) in terms of accuracy, TNR, TPR, and NPV.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report any formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Performance comparisons were made using absolute metric values (Accuracy, TPR, TNR, PPV, NPV) and percentage improvements between the proposed model and comparative techniques.

Dermatology Dataset.notes: The paper evaluates the proposed FGOA_CNN-based TL with VGG-16 against comparative techniques (LSI with CFS, DOLHGS, Multi-scale structure, RegNetY-320) and optimization algorithms (WOA, EHO, LOA, GOA) using varying training data percentages and k-fold cross-validation values. The maximal results reported are based on 90% training data and k-fold=9.

architecture_used.main_model: Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) based Transfer Learning with Visual Geometric Group-16 (VGG-16)
architecture_used.pretraining: Transfer Learning (TL) used to initialize CNN hyperparameters with VGG-16

architecture_details.hyperparameter_initialization: Initialized by VGG-16 via Transfer Learning and trained by FGOA

conclusions: The proposed Fractional Gazelle Optimization Algorithm (FGOA) integrated with Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) based Transfer Learning using VGG-16 (FGOA_CNN based TL with VGG-16) is highly superior for primary skin cancer detection compared to comparative techniques (LSI with CFS, DOLHGS, Multi-scale structure, RegNetY-320) and other optimization algorithms (WOA, EHO, LOA, GOA).

The proposed model achieved maximal performance metrics of 92.65% accuracy, 91.48% True Positive Rate (TPR), 93.56% True Negative Rate (TNR), 90.77% Positive Predictive Value (PPV), and 90.35% Negative Predictive Value (NPV) on the Dermatology Dataset.

The integration of Fractional Calculus (FC) into the Gazelle Optimization Algorithm (GOA) to create FGOA effectively optimizes feature selection and CNN hyperparameters, leading to enhanced detection efficiency.
The proposed method demonstrates robustness across varying training data percentages (70%, 80%, 90%) and k-fold cross-validation values (k=7, 8, 9), consistently outperforming baseline techniques.
Algorithmic analysis indicates that the proposed FGOA is sensitive to swarm size, with optimal accuracy (92.64%) observed at a swarm size of 90 compared to other optimization algorithms.
important_remarks: The study relies on a small, non-image Dermatology Dataset from the UCI repository containing clinical and histopathological features (34 hyperparameters, 33 linear features) rather than raw dermoscopy or histology images, limiting its applicability to computer vision-based diagnostic tools.

The dataset size and composition are not explicitly reported (total count, class distribution, or train/test split sizes are missing), preventing assessment of sample size adequacy or class imbalance.
No external validation was performed; the model was evaluated solely on the single UCI Dermatology Dataset, raising concerns about generalizability to other populations or data distributions.
The paper does not report statistical significance tests (e.g., p-values) for performance comparisons, relying only on absolute metric values and percentage improvements.

The task is binary classification (cancerous vs. non-cancerous) despite the introduction discussing specific subtypes (melanoma, basal cell carcinoma, squamous cell carcinoma), which may not align with the clinical complexity of multi-class skin cancer diagnosis.
Data preprocessing involved missing value imputation and linear normalization, but details on the imputation method and normalization parameters are not provided, posing reproducibility risks.

The proposed Fractional Gazelle Optimization Algorithm (FGOA) is a novel heuristic; its computational complexity and training time are not analyzed, which is critical for clinical deployment feasibility.
The study acknowledges future work on large-scale datasets and IoT environments, indicating current results are preliminary and not yet validated in real-world or large-scale clinical settings.
